TECHNICAL ANALYSIS

Advanced Knowledge

Backtesting a strategy

💡 Backtesting is an essential process in trading that allows you to evaluate how a particular strategy would have performed using historical market data. By simulating trades on past price movements, backtesting helps you understand the strengths and weaknesses of your approach before risking real capital. This way, you can determine if a strategy aligns with your trading style and risk tolerance, and make necessary adjustments to improve its effectiveness.

In my case, I generally avoid scalping or very short-term trades. Instead, I focus on HTF levels such as daily or weekly charts because trading for me is a complementary activity, not a full-time job. I prefer not to be glued to the screen all day or constantly chasing every small price movement. Instead, I build my strategy around significant zones on larger time frames, which tend to offer more reliable signals and require less frequent monitoring.

This approach allows me to balance trading with other commitments and maintain a healthy lifestyle, while still capturing meaningful market moves. Structuring my strategy this way reflects my personal goals and time constraints, emphasizing quality setups over quantity. I start by writing out the trading rules in plain language. In this content I will show you the process, so you’ll have a guideline on how to do so based on your style, strategy and attitude.

1. Define your strategy clearly

  • What triggers my entry?

My entry is justified by a clean BOS above/below a key swing confirmed by an HTF closure (which, to me, is at least a 1D one) as this shows me the intention from one side or another to take the counterpart dominance. In the meantime, I start searching for a potential PB usually toward a freshly formed OB (EQ of the candle) or toward, if created with the impulse above/below the swing, the FVG. (premium/discount depending on the trade).

When/if the price comes back down/up in my region of interest, I pull the trigger after having evaluated closures within the level in order to not get caught in a fake trade and after checking if majors are aligned. Sometimes the price reacts in a sharp manner without giving me the time to wait and act, sometimes it does but this doesn't change the fact that I don't chase it and

I prefer to miss out, waiting for another opportunity.

Setting LOs? It could be valid, but when trading with leverage it holds more risk so I keep it at low risk preferring to operate with MOs. Sometimes I won't be able to stack all confluences but this doesn't make a potential setup invalid, it simply makes it more risky so in this case I will look for at least a ranging condition in BTC to take individual altcoin levels.

  • What triggers my exit?

My exit is justified by previous OBs and significant liquidity zones toward crucial FVGs and regardless, are based on premium/discount depending on the trade.I have all the exit levels already plot in my charts categorized by "partial" and "more consistent" ones where I will scale out different %s depending on the strength of the zones (HTF, significant key fibs/macro ones) so in this manner my brain is not forced to chase the dopamine coming from impulsive PA, it has "barriers" where to stop it.

Price hits my levels? I'm selling no matter what. These regions are often in alignment with fib extensions which play a vital role in my strategy.

For spot bags I have pre-set TP levels that will be automatically triggered (it's my decision to leave spot bags on an exchange, I'm aware of the risks).

Independently, if majors are at risk, I will not be focused on individual altcoins targets but I will start to cut my positions or at least trailing them along.

I'm humble by nature, so this helps me a lot to be satisfied with what made glorifying each dollar I extract from the market.

Example here →

  • Mental traps to avoid?

There's no room for "I want to go big on this because I have strong conviction" or "That person said that this won't play out". I play my game, with my rules and my invalidations and if I make a mistake, it is being made with my brain so I can learn from.

2. Collect relevant historical data

I make sure the historical data I’m using is clean, accurate, and representative of the market conditions I expect to trade in. (I use SMC so, as explained above, I rely on all the concepts around so FVGs, OBs etc). Despite their relevance across the board, I scroll through individual assets (spending hours and hours at the charts) in order to see how they respond to levels.

For example, a coin like BNB has responded extremely well to its imbalances, demands and supply zones wicking minimally and therefore offering safer trades compared to other assets that wicked way more.

I’ll often spend weeks observing how an asset behaves at premium/discount zones, how often it gives inducement before entries, and whether the reactions are sharp and controlled or sloppy and volatile.

  • Does it respect OBs without deep wicks?
  • Does it sweep liquidity before breaking structure, or does it randomly break levels?
  • Are its FVGs respected regularly or ignored?

Some charts look good in theory, but when you start monitoring them day by day, you realize they’re erratic, they overshoot OBs, or they consolidate too messily to read clean intent. Eventually, I narrow down keeping a focused watchlist, maybe 8 to 12 names, and treat them like my own personal case studies.

3. Run the backtest with discipline

This is where most traders, including myself at times, fall into subtle traps. It’s easy to say you’re backtesting a strategy, but what many are actually doing is cherry-picking, adjusting, or subconsciously avoiding trades that don’t “look right.” That’s not testing the system, that’s protecting your ego.

When I run a backtest, I force myself to follow the rules as they are written, not as I wish they were in the moment. This means every trade that meets the criteria, even if it looks sketchy, uncertain, or emotionally uncomfortable, gets taken. Why? Because I’m not trying to prove I’m a good trader during backtesting. I’m trying to see, in a raw and honest way, how the strategy performs when I stick to it without interference.

The temptation to skip a trade after a few losses, or to “tweak” an entry slightly to avoid an uncomfortable setup, is huge and it’s often unconscious. I’ve caught myself hesitating on trades that looked messy only to realize I was making decisions based on fear of being wrong, not based on the system I’m testing. That completely ruins the purpose of the backtest.

4. Evaluate with metrics

After the backtest is complete, I don’t just glance at the final profit and call it a day. I go much deeper, because a profitable strategy can still be dangerous if it produces that profit in an unstable or unsustainable way. That’s why I focus on risk-adjusted returns, not just how much the system made, but how it made it.

One of the most important things I look at is the Sharpe ratio.

It tells me whether the strategy’s returns were smooth and consistent relative to the risk it took. A system that made $10,000 but had massive swings and emotional rollercoasters along the way might look good on paper, but it could be a nightmare to trade live. The Sharpe ratio helps expose that.

I also examine the maximum drawdown, the largest drop the equity curve experienced from a high to a low point. This is crucial. I ask myself: "would I have had the mental strength to keep executing the strategy through that drawdown in real time? Because if the backtest shows a 25% drawdown, and I know I’d emotionally tap out at 10%, then the strategy isn’t truly viable for me, no matter how profitable it ends up."

The win/loss ratio gives more "color" to the story. A system might win only 40% of the time, but if the winners are 3x the size of the losers, it can still be incredibly profitable. Still, I need to see that this dynamic is consistent, not based on a few lucky big wins skewing the results.

Finally, I study the equity curve. I don’t just want to see it rising: I want to see how it behaves.

  • Is it smooth, steady, controlled?
  • Or is it jagged, erratic, and driven by a few massive spikes?

The shape of the equity curve says a lot about the psychological experience of trading the system. A strategy with a messy curve might technically “work,” but if it’s emotionally exhausting, it won’t last.

5. Test out of sample

After completing a backtest, I don’t immediately assume the strategy is solid just because it performed well on past data. That initial performance could easily be the result of curve-fitting where the strategy has been unintentionally tailored to fit historical patterns too perfectly. To avoid this trap, I run what’s called an out of sample test.

Out of sample testing means validating the strategy on fresh data it hasn’t seen before, data that wasn’t used during the original testing or tweaking phase. If, for example, I tested the strategy on price action from January to June, I’ll now run it on July to September without changing a single rule. The idea is to see whether the strategy holds up in different market conditions, or whether its success was limited to one specific stretch of time.

This step matters because markets change. They go through different cycles: trending, consolidating, or ranging. A strategy that worked in a trending environment might completely fall apart when the market becomes choppy unless it’s built on principles that adapt well across conditions. Testing it out of sample helps reveal whether the strategy is genuinely strong or just overfitted to the past.

6. Journal every iteration

Whenever I try different versions of my trading strategy, I make sure to write down everything: what I tested, how it went, and any changes I made. Doing this keeps me honest with myself because I can’t just ignore the losses or only remember the wins. It helps me really learn from both my successes and my mistakes.

By keeping this record, I understand what works, what doesn’t, and why, so I can keep improving my strategy over time instead of just guessing or relying on my memory. It’s how I stay organized and grow as a trader.

7. Gut check and “forward testing”

At the end of the day, I always check in with myself and ask: "Does this strategy really match how I understand and feel about the market?". Because no matter how good a system looks on paper, if it doesn’t align with my own market view or personality, I’m unlikely to stick with it when things get tough.

I also ask myself if I truly have the discipline to follow this strategy live: can I manage my emotions, resist the urge to deviate, and stick to the plan even during losing streaks?

If I’m confident in both how I think about the market and my ability to trade the strategy consistently, that’s when I take the next step. I’ll start forward-testing it by paper trading or using a small live account with real money, so I can experience the pressures of real-time trading without risking too much. This way, I can see how it feels in practice and make sure it really works for me before fully committing.
